Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Jones produces "All Things Considered," a local Minnesota version of the national program from NPR in Washington, D.C. "My program is local to Minnesota," he says. "I'm not interested in pitches about national guests or national topics unless there is a direct, specific connection to Minnesota." The program's content is news and topics of current interest to people living in Minnesota such as arts, culture, technology, politics, the environment, economics and education. "We don't talk to pundits or people with self-help books," he says. "We talk to newsmakers and people affected by the news." The best pitches are about a specific person, event or organization that's doing something new or something indicative of a noteworthy trend. If you provide him with an exclusive, offer a real person or give him a story with a beginning, a middle and an end that speaks to a larger topic, he'll listen. A pitch offering an exclusive interview with the new director of a local unit of the national park service resulted in a story. Contact by email.

Contact Profile: Langdon is a General Assignment reporter.   He provides morning news for WMNN, as well as being a back-up sports reporter. He is the morning drive-time news anchor for the Minnesota News Network, and is heard on over 50 stations across Minnesota, as well as WMNN.   Langdon put himself on the line during the Animal Genetics conference protest in the summer of 2000, when he was pepper-sprayed while covering the action live for WMNN. As a General Assignment reporter Langdon has covered a wide range of news from the tragic death of Timberwolves guard Malik Sealy to the opening of a Spanish-language cultural center in south Minneapolis.   Langdon is often on assignment for the WMNN sports department, covering the Timberwolves, Vikings, Twins and Wild. He's a self-described sports nut who lists one of his best jobs as that of public address announcer for the Saint Cloud River Bats of the Northwoods League.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Produces several current events/talk programs including: "Tim Russell," "WCCO Midday Live" and "Al Malmberg." She wants guests who are "good storytellers [who] give capsulized answers," she says, and "experts who can discuss current news events. I want someone who can talk about business ethics. Please don't call me to pitch an idea--send it in writing, then give me a day or two to look at it," she adds. No self-help, financial success or new age healing stories. Would like to see less pitches regarding "diets, motivational, business; we don't like books." Send a press release, press kit, product sample, backgrounder or story pitch. Calls are best Mon.-Fri., 10 a.m.-4 p.m., CST.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Fields pitches for a news-interview program focusing on St. Cloud and MN. "I don't want to pigeonhole us--we cover everything from pocketbook issues to human interest to Congress, the legislature and law," Voss says. "The focus has to be as local as possible, and it can be quite difficult to localize some of these stories." So if you want to pitch a retail market analyst, be sure they can discuss holiday shopping in Minneapolis rather than New York. His ideal guest is a local newsmaker who is comfortable speaking on the radio, but he welcomes pitches about visiting authors or other dignitaries. Give two or three days' notice for time-sensitive stories. Pitch by fax or email, with a St. Cloud dateline in the subject field. Don't follow-up.
